hq: 142 Minna Street, Floor 2, San Francisco, CA 94105
signing_authority: Rohan Kalahasty, CEO — actual signature present (not a placeholder)
_flag: FIRST_REAL_CUSTOMER — This is the first non-Delve company in the dataset. a37 Inc. is a real company with a real CEO signature (Rohan Kalahasty), real HQ address (142 Minna Street, SF), and a real network diagram. The report uses the identical 58-page Delve SOC 2 Type 1 template as DOC 6 (Delve/AWS/T1) but with a37-specific content properly substituted. Some yellow Delve template instruction highlights remain in the Products description section only — otherwise properly completed. AWS as subservice organization.

system_description.overview: a37 Inc. provides Forge, an AI-native DevOps workspace. Forge enables software engineering teams to automate complex DevOps workflows using YAML-based workflow definitions triggered by events, API calls, or Slack commands. The platform is designed as read-only by default with approval gates required for system-changing actions.

products.Forge — AI-native DevOps Workspace: YAML-based workflow automation platform for DevOps teams. Workflows triggered by events, API calls, or Slack commands. Read-only access to connected systems by default; system-changing actions require explicit approval gates. Integrates with existing engineering toolchains to automate complex multi-step DevOps operations.

infrastructure.cloud_provider: Amazon Web Services / AWS (carve-out — AWS data center controls excluded from scope)
regions: Not specified — AWS regions not named in report
infrastructure.physical_presence: None — no physical offices; all data processed on cloud services. HQ listed as 142 Minna Street, Floor 2, San Francisco, CA 94105 but report states a37 does not have an office (A1.2 exclusion).
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ — DR plan references spinning up multiple servers across all availability zones in AWS (p.56)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: System hosted in AWS in a virtual private cloud (VPC) environment with segmented VPCs and access control lists (ACLs). Network segmentation implemented to separate systems and data based on sensitivity and criticality.
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management.
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS deployed via AWS to identify and protect against threats.
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access done through a bastion host with a virtual private network (VPN). VPC VPN connections shown in network diagram.
network_architecture.tls: TLS with certificates from established third-party certificate authority
network_architecture.content_filtering: All systems restricted to internet with content filtering system routed through proxy server
network_architecture.topology: REAL network diagram present on p.17 — complex multi-component AWS architecture diagram showing actual production infrastructure. Not a placeholder. Internet -> VPN/Internet Gateway -> AWS VPC with multiple availability zones. Bastion host for remote access. Specific AWS services and components visible in diagram.

application_architecture.pattern: Not explicitly stated — YAML-based workflow engine suggests event-driven architecture. DevOps automation platform with API and Slack triggers.
frameworks: Not disclosed — YAML-based workflow definitions for end users
compute: AWS virtual servers (cloud architecture including database, networking devices, virtual servers)
application_architecture.api_style: API-triggered workflows (REST implied from event/API trigger description); Slack integration
application_architecture.environments: Separate development, test, staging, and production environments. Changes tested in separate development or test environment before implementation.

databases: Not named — generic reference to cloud database infrastructure
data_storage.backup_frequency: Not explicitly stated; backup policy and procedures referenced but no frequency confirmed (Type 1 — no test results)

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: AWS (passwords controlled through 'Password policy of AWS' per p.23; Active Directory referenced for server and database accounts)
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for user accounts with administrative access to production platform; MFA required for source code tool access
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination
authentication_access_control.password_policy: AWS password policy: periodic forced changes, password expiry and complexity requirements; accounts disabled after limited unsuccessful logon attempts; guest and anonymous logins not allowed; unattended desktops locked on inactivity; biometrics as unlock alternative

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service used to encrypt data at rest
encryption.key_management: AWS KMS (cloud provider key management service) — access to production environment access keys restricted to authorized individuals

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Not named — 'source code management tool' referenced with MFA access
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured to ensure every merge request to move change to production requires additional approval. Developers cannot make changes to production without additional approval.
ci_cd_devops.iac: Baseline configurations retained within infrastructure as code; configuration management tool used for implementation and rollback
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: CTO/Management set up automatic patch management; monthly patch management reviews; workstations configured for automatic updates and scanned daily for patch compliance

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not named — 'monitoring tool' that generates alerts on predefined thresholds
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Not named — logging enabled for administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions, changes to functions, security configurations, permissions, and roles
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events

third_party_services.purpose: Cloud infrastructure / data center services (subservice organization, carve-out)

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and Malware protection software installed on all production servers (continuous scanning, weekly definition updates) and Mac workstations/laptops (daily updates from vendor website).
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications; internal IT Infrastructure team performs periodic scans
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annually by independent third-party; high/critical findings remediated in timely manner
security_tools.cybersecurity_insurance: Not explicitly mentioned in this report

bcdr.daily_backups: Not confirmed — backup policy exists but frequency not stated (Type 1 report, no test results)

compliance_controls._note: SOC 2 Type 1 — design of controls only, no tests of operating effectiveness performed. Section 4 starts p.26 — no 'tested' results column.
excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — AWS is responsible for ensuring the physical security of its data center
A1.2 — a37 does not have an office; environmental security managed by building management/AWS
Processing Integrity criteria — deemed not relevant to system
Privacy criteria — deemed not relevant to system
notable_controls: CC8.1.2 — Branch protection rules enforced; merge requires additional approval
CC8.1.3 — Source code changes logged, time-stamped, attributed in SCM tool with MFA access
CC4.1.1 — Annual external penetration testing by independent third party
CC6.1.5 — Cloud provider KMS for encryption key management
CC7.4.1 — BCDR policy with annual testing confirmed
CC9.2.1 — Vendor management policy; annual SOC report review; quarterly risk assessments for critical vendors without SOC reports

infrastructure.rationale: AWS VPC with multi-AZ confirmed (DR text p.56), IDS/IPS via AWS, VPN via bastion host, IaC. REAL network diagram present (p.17) showing actual AWS architecture — significant differentiator vs all other Delve reports. No named AWS regions or specific services in text. Single cloud provider.

application_architecture.rationale: Forge is described as YAML-based workflow automation with event/API/Slack triggers and approval gates — more product specificity than Delve's own reports. Separate environments confirmed, branch protection confirmed, IaC referenced. No languages, frameworks, or specific compute types disclosed. Type 1 provides no test evidence.

data_layer.rationale: Backup policy exists, multi-AZ referenced, data classification and retention policies confirmed, per-tenant segregation confirmed. Backup frequency not confirmed (Type 1). No specific database technologies named.

security_posture.rationale: Annual external pen test, monthly vuln scans, IDS/IPS via AWS, firewalls, MFA enforced, AV (weekly prod / daily workstations), quarterly access reviews, 1-business-day termination SLA, AWS KMS. No WAF, no SIEM, no cybersecurity insurance explicitly mentioned. Type 1 provides no test evidence of controls operating.

devops_maturity.rationale: IaC referenced, branch protection confirmed, separate environments, emergency change process, monthly patch management. Change management: approved by IS team, sign-off from requestor after implementation. No CI/CD tool named, no SCM platform named, no container orchestration. YAML-based workflow automation is the product itself — suggests sophisticated DevOps capability not reflected in controls documentation.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy exists, annual review and testing confirmed, multi-AZ failover confirmed in AWS. No RTO/RPO disclosed. No multi-region failover. Daily backup frequency not confirmed in Type 1.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Only AWS explicitly named as subservice organization. No other third-party vendors disclosed. Single critical cloud dependency. Noteworthy gap: Forge integrates with third-party engineering tools (Slack, source control, etc.) but none are listed as subservice organizations.

overall.rationale: First real non-Delve customer in dataset. Properly completed report with real CEO signature, real HQ address, real network diagram — higher confidence than Delve's own reports. SOC 2 Type 1 limitation applies (design only). Template is identical 58-page Delve structure as DOC 6. Yellow highlights remain only in Products description section. AWS is enterprise-appropriate. Product (Forge) is a DevOps automation platform with inherently interesting security implications — YAML workflows with approval gates is a noteworthy security design. Limited tech stack disclosure keeps overall score moderate.

red_flags: SOC 2 Type 1 only — no operating effectiveness tested; provides significantly less assurance than Type 2.
Some yellow Delve template instruction highlights remain in the Products description section — partially completed template.
No specific AWS services named (no EC2, RDS, S3, Lambda, etc.) despite having a real network diagram.
No subservice organizations listed beyond AWS — Forge integrates with Slack and likely other third-party systems that should be disclosed.
Availability and Confidentiality criteria not in scope — Security only.
yellow_flags: No CI/CD tool named despite source code management being referenced.
No SIEM or log aggregation platform named.
Only one named subservice organization (AWS) — third-party integrations (Slack, SCM tools) not disclosed as vendors.
Audit firm 'Accorp Partners' — less established than Big 4 or major SOC auditors.
Security implications of Forge's core design (YAML workflows that can execute system-changing actions) not explicitly addressed in controls.
green_flags: Real CEO signature (Rohan Kalahasty) — report is properly executed, not a placeholder.
Real HQ address: 142 Minna Street, Floor 2, San Francisco, CA 94105.
REAL network diagram on p.17 — complex multi-component AWS architecture, not a placeholder.
AWS as cloud provider — enterprise-standard for compliance workloads.
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party confirmed.
Multi-AZ deployment with BCDR annual testing confirmed.
MFA enforced for administrative and production access.
1-business-day access revocation SLA for terminated employees.
IaC used for infrastructure management with rollback capability.
Branch protection rules enforced with required approvals for production merges.
BCDR plan with annual testing confirmed.
Vendor management policy with quarterly risk assessments for critical vendors without SOC reports.
Forge product design includes approval gates for system-changing actions — security-by-design principle.
key_observations: a37 Inc. is the first real non-Delve customer in this dataset — all prior reports (DOCs 1-6) were Delve's own product across different cloud providers and report types.

Product differentiation: Forge is a YAML-based DevOps workflow automation platform with event/API/Slack triggers. The 'read-only by default, approval gates for system-changing actions' design is a notable security feature that should be highlighted in any due diligence.

Report structure is byte-for-byte identical to DOC 6 (Delve/AWS/SOC2T1) in template — same 58 pages, same section layout, same control numbering. This confirms Delve's compliance platform generates reports from a fixed template.
The presence of a real network diagram (p.17) on a real AWS architecture vs the placeholder diagrams in all Delve-own reports is the single most meaningful differentiator in terms of report quality.
Org chart: CEO (Rohan Kalahasty) → CPO/COO/CTO → AI/ML Team/DevOps Team/Engineering Team — confirms dedicated AI/ML and DevOps capacity.
Change management: Changes approved by IS team, with sign-off from requestor after implementation — slightly more specific than boilerplate.
DR text (p.56): 'a37 has implemented logical controls to safeguard against interruption of service... spinning up multiple servers across all availability zones in AWS' — AWS-confirmed multi-AZ.
Password policy: 'Password policy of AWS' (p.23) — same language as DOC 6 (Delve AWS T1), confirming AWS as identity infrastructure.
Accorp Partners license PAC-FIRM-LIC-47383 — same license number as all Delve reports, suggesting Accorp Partners exclusively audits Delve-generated reports.
AV definitions: weekly for production servers, daily for Mac workstations — consistent with DOC 6 and DOC 1.
